sources.notes: Queries ask for the minimum value after replacing ? with bounded counts of X, V, I in a string-valued Roman-like scoring rule.
Official tutorial code precomputes run effects of ? blocks and answers each query by greedily allocating V/X replacements.

annotation.summary: Given a string over X,V,I,? and many replacement-count queries, minimize the Roman-like string value after assigning all ? within per-letter limits.
annotation.constraints: Sum of n and q is up to 3e5, so each query must be answered in O(1) or logarithmic time after preprocessing the fixed string.
annotation.core_idea: Normalize X as V plus a fixed contribution, set all ? to I as a baseline, decompose consecutive ? runs together with adjacent I/V effects, and precompute how many non-I replacements decrease or increase the value. Each query then greedily uses required non-I letters, first consuming replacements that reduce subtractive I interactions.
annotation.complexity: O(n + q) per test case after run preprocessing.

solution_variants.name: run-contribution-greedy
solution_variants.summary: Precompute aggregate contribution classes of ? runs and answer each query from the number of forced V/X replacements.

tags.evidence: The solution is driven by maintaining the delta from a fixed all-I baseline and applying precomputed contribution buckets per query.
For each query, the required non-I replacements are greedily assigned to the most beneficial run-effect classes first.
The preprocessing groups consecutive ? positions and accounts for their boundary characters.
tags.description: Techniques that compress or reason over maximal runs of equal characters in a string.
tags.created_reason: Existing string tags do not distinguish run/block decomposition, which is the reusable string-specific technique here.

tags.evidence: The mathematical idea is simple after preprocessing, but correct handling of boundaries and query counts is implementation-heavy.
